Assist the barangays or cluster of barangays in the establishment of MRF, allocate funds as necessary, and encourage resource conservation and recovery through waste reduction to comply with Section 32 of RA No. 9003 and DILG Circular No. 2009-168;
Ensure that the SWMP for 2025-2034 is compliant with the provisions of RA No. 9003 and pertinent laws, rules, and regulations on solid waste management; and
Henceforth, ensure compliance with RA No. 9003, DILG MC No. 2009-168, DILG Circular No. 2009-168, and other relevant laws, rules and regulations on the implementation of solid waste management program.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the LSB to:

As an additional control in ensuring the non-inclusion of expenditure items the counterpart DepEd has provided budget for, secure a Certification from the Accountant of the counterpart DepEd that an expenditure item to be charged against the SEF has not been provided budget for by the counterpart DepEd;

Ensure 100 percent accomplishment of SEF PPAs considering funds are available thereto; and

Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC Nos. 1, 
s. 2017, 1, s. 2020, and 2, 
s. 2020, relative to the utilization of SEF.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor issued Memorandum Order No. 08 on April 11, 2023 reiterating guidelines under JCs on the SEF Utilization. The memorandum reminded the City Budget Department (CBD) and the LSB that the SEF provides the source of funds for the supplementary annual needs for the operation and maintenance of public schools within the city through the annual SEF budget. Also, the allocation for the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF shall be net of budgetary provision for the same or related items in the budget for the DepEd. Lastly, the memorandum reiterated Item 4.0 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s.2017 and Item 1.0 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s.2020 which listed the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF.

observations.recommendation:
We commended Management for its compliance to the requirement to insure its properties in CY 2021 with the GIF as administered by the GSIS following RA No. 656, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 and COA Circular No. 2018-002. However, we recommended that Management:
Use the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2021, in preparing the PIF for submission to COA and GSIS not later than April 30, 2022;
Cause the appraisal of the insurable properties and other assets, if necessary. For this purpose, an in-house appraisal shall be sufficient if the property or insurable interest has a value of P10.0 million and below; and
Henceforth, ensure that all insurable interests and properties are insured under Republic Act No. 656, as amended by PD No. 245 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of payment of salaries, overtime pay or other incentives through cash advances and facilitate payments of salaries, allowances and benefits through the employee's accounts with LBP; and
Henceforth, comply with Section 124 of PD No. 1145, Item 2 of COA Circular No. 76-26A dated April 6, 1976, Item 4.1.2 of BTr Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019, and Items 7.1 and 7.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We commended the City for allocating more than the statutory requirement of 5% of its annual budget for LDRRM responsive PPAs and utilizing 95.476% of its LDRRMF. However, we recommended that the City:
Determine the nature of the LDRRM PPAs in the continuing appropriations and evaluate its implementation, and realign priorities, if necessary, to maximize the utilization of available funds to address the adverse effects of the COVID-19 pandemic; and
Continue to strictly comply with the provisions of the RA No. 10121 and its IRR and the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 in implementing LDRRM PPAs.

We commended the City for allocating one percent (1%) or P10,349,982.00 of the IRA in CY 2021 for the strengthening and implementation of the programs of the LCPC. However, we recommended that Management:
Fully utilize its budget appropriation for LCPC to ensure that all Programs, Projects and Activities outlined in the Annual Investment Plan for Children are implemented; and
Henceforth, ensure compliance with Section 15 of RA No. 9344, the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006, and City Ordinance No. 706, s. 2018.

The Accounting Department has already assigned a personnel-in-charge to prioritize the reconciliation of reciprocal accounts to prevent further errors and omissions.
observations.reason: CAD has not reconciled the difference between the Due to and Due from Other Funds account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant submit the required supporting documents for the payment of operational expenses totaling P9,176,000.00 to avoid suspension of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that they would advise all concerned officials and employees to ensure that all disbursement of funds is adequately supported with the appropriate required supporting documents and observe strict compliance with R.A 9184 and its IRR and COA Circular 2012-001.
observations.reason: CAD has not submitted the required supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The City Assessor and other concerned personnel to provide clear policy on the sale of idle lands to collect the 5% Ad Valorem Tax before registering the transactions and perfecting the transfer of ownership to the new owner.




The City Treasurer collect the additional Ad Valorem Tax totaling to at least P49,127,142.
observations.managementAction:
The City Assessor made a written request to the Sangguniang Panlungsod to amend Chapter 12 of City Ordinance No. 484, S-2011 to make the provisions on idle lands optional rather than mandatory.
Management forwarded a copy of the list of idle lands to the City Auditor.

The City Treasurer (CT) was given a copy of the updated record of idle lands.
observations.reason:
The amendments in the City Revenue Code that will cover the Ad Valorem Tax has not been acted upon by the Sangguniang Panlungsod,  




The collection has not been made pending the amendment of the City Ordinance.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Management require:

The City Accountant and the City Treasurer to accelerate measures to locate the supporting documents and continue the review and reconciliation procedures pertaining to the significant amounts of cash reconciling items, unidentified/ unreconciled balances and dormant accounts, so that appropriate reclassification to the PPSAS accounts may be undertaken; and

The City Accountant review and analyze the unidentified/ unreconciled balance of P39,056,910.26 and initiate appropriate actions in order to locate the missing records and documents pertaining to long outstanding balances for proper adjustment to PPSAS accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Other Assets account has been reclassified to their original accounts in accordance with PPSAS. Reconciliation and adjusting journal entries were recorded to correct the balances in the current year.

The Management created a special team led by CTD and CAD employees which focused on searching and identifying documents of prior year’s transactions of the City. Thereafter, reconciliation and adjustments were recorded in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require:

     a. The GSD to coordinate with the CAD to expedite  the  reconciliation
        between the RPCPPE  and  accounting  records  to  account  for  the
        discrepancy amounting to P575,561,376.62;

     b. The Appraisal Committee to appraise all existing PPEs without costs
        for subsequent recording in the books;

     c. The CAD to –

         • Analyze and investigate the noted discrepancies between the book
           balance  and  the  RPCPPE  to  fully   identify   the   errors/deficiencies
           and make the necessary  adjustments  as  appropriate;


         •  Exercise  prudence  in  the  preparation  of  the  Lapsing
           Schedule by indicating specific identification of PPE items
           for accurate computation of depreciation; and


         • Prepare the necessary  adjusting  entries  to  correct  the
           erroneous    recording    of    depreciation,     supplies,
           misclassified PPE items and undelivered  PPEs.  Thereafter,
           ensure that all PPEs were properly recorded  based  on  the
           nature of the procured items and recognized  in  the  books
           upon actual delivery and acceptance by the City.

We recommend that the LCE direct the concerned local officials  to
          provide funding or budget for  the  transfer  of  land  titles  to
          reflect absolute ownership over the property so that the  interest
          of  the  City  against  third  party  claims,  if  any,  is  fully
          protected. Also, instruct the Appraisal Committee to appraise  the
          values of  the  29  TCTs  to  establish  the  fair  value  of  the
          properties for proper recording by the CAD in the books.

We recommended that the LCE direct:


          a. The Appraisal Committee (AC) to determine the appraised values
             of the 18 TCTs not included in the RPCPPE and  19  parcels  of
             land without cost;


          b. The City Assessor prepare the corresponding tax declaration of
             the properties based on the appraised values determined by the
             AC;

          c.     The City Treasurer to secure copies of the  original  TCTs
             of the 41 lots owned by the City and provide a list of TCTs to
             the Inventory Committee for inclusion in the RPCPPE;

          d.  The  Inventory  Committee  ensure  that  all  properties  are
             included in the RPCPPE; and

          e.     The CAD prepare the adjusting  entries  to  recognize  the
             appraised values of all  unrecorded  land  in  the  books  and
             maintain PPE ledger cards.

We recommended that the City Accountant  and  the  City  Treasurer
          locate the supporting documents of prior years’ reconciling  items
          and effect the appropriate adjustments in their respective records
          and cashbooks to establish the correct balance of the Cash - Local
          Treasury  and   other   affected   accounts.   Likewise,   current
          reconciling items should be regularly  adjusted  in  the  SLs  and
          Cashbooks.

We recommended that the LCE require:


          a. The City Accountant to coordinate with the  City  Treasurer  in
             securing the pertinent documents relative  to  the  reconciling
             items from the depository  bank  and  thereafter,  prepare  the
             necessary adjustments in the books.


          b. The City Treasurer to request  timely  submission  of  debit/credit
             memos (DMs/CMs) with the depository bank;


          c.   The CAD and the CTD to exert  concerted  efforts  to  review,
             analyze and reconcile their respective records to come up  with
             the correct balances; and


          d.  The City Accountant inform in writing the  City  Treasurer  of
             the reconciling items to take up adjustments in the cashbook.

We recommended that:


          a. The LCE –

             • Ensure strict compliance  with  applicable  laws,  rules  and
               regulations on the grant, utilization and liquidation of CA;


             • Require AOs concerned to submit immediately  all  documentary
               requirements for the liquidation of their  cash  advances  to
               avoid any administrative  or  criminal  action  that  may  be
               instituted against them;


             • Instruct the AOs, who are still connected with the City and
               with unliquidated CAs, to avail of the remedies laid down  by
               the law, rules and regulations.


          b. The City Accountant –


             • Establish the nature of  the  prior  years’  cash  advances
               and review past records and documents to determine  propriety
               of recording and  exhaust  all  possible  means  to  locate  and
               collect the amount due from those who are no longer  in  the
               service; and


             • Review and analyze the negative balances in the  sub-ledgers
               of  the  accountable  officers   and   make   the   necessary
               adjustments, if any.

We recommended that:


          a. The City Accountant –


             • Recognize all supplies and  materials  purchased  during  the
               year as inventory and record the related expense account upon
               issuance or distribution;


             • Refrain from charging of previous year’s transactions to  the
               current year expenses pursuant to IPSAS 12.


          b. The Inventory Committee  conduct  periodic  physical  count  of
             supplies and materials, prepare the RPCI and submit to CAD  for
             proper reconciliation of the account; and


          c. The GSD prepare and submit to CAD the  consolidated  report  of
             SSMI based on RIS, on a monthly  basis,  for  proper  recording
             of  the  expenses in the books.

We recommended that the City Accountant:

           a. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries  to  derecognize  the
              undelivered items booked as PPE and the corresponding payable
              accounts totaling P69,4543,110.00;


           b. Review, analyze and validate undocumented  dormant  and  long
              outstanding accounts, and if warranted,  adjust  the  AP  and
              Government Equity Accounts;

           c. Ensure  that  no  obligation  is  booked  unless  supported   with
              sufficient  documentation  and  approved  by  the  authorized
              officials/ representatives; and

           d.  Record  obligations  based  on  the  actual  nature  of  the
              transaction in compliance  with  COA  Circular  2015-009  and
              reclassify the unpaid salaries  and  wages,  allowances,  and
              bonuses totaling P47,656,870.79 and terminal  leave  benefits
              amounting to P1,211,714.18 to Due to Officers  and  Employees
              and Leave Benefits Payable, respectively.

We recommended that:

          a. The LCE


             • Initiate the return of any unused balances for the  completed
               projects to the  SAs  or  file  a  request  to  the  SAs  for
               authority to use the unexpended trust fund balance for  other
               City projects,  if  the  disposition  thereof  has  not  been
               provided in the Memorandum of Agreement;


             •  Ensure  that  trust  funds   are   used   only   to   pay   for
               obligations/projects for which they were granted;


          b. The City Accountant


             • Intensify its efforts to reconcile its books  with  the  SAs.
               Consequently, make the necessary adjustments, if any, for  the
               proper accounting and liquidation  thereof,  and  henceforth,
               submit the FURs to the SAs upon completion of  the  project
               for proper recording in the books of the latter;


             • Reclassify the  receipts  totaling  P16,784,596.66  to   Other
               Deferred  Credits.  Thereafter,  record  receipts/obligations
               based  on  the  actual  nature  of  the  transaction  and  in
               compliance with IPSAS;

          c. The City Accountant,  in  coordination  with  the  City  Treasurer,
             undertake a thorough analysis and assessment  on  the  validity
             and propriety of these dormant accounts; and


          d. The City Accountant, together with the City Treasurer  and  the
             City Engineer, perform periodic monitoring of  the  status  and
             payment of the projects under the Trust Fund.

We recommended that:


          a. The Head of the  MCMC  coordinate  with  BIZBOX  Inc.,  through
             Philhealth, to address the deficiencies noted in their  system.
             Thereafter, submit a Schedule of PhilHealth Claims to the  CAD,
             on a weekly basis,  covering  all  transmitted  claims  to  the
             Electronic  Claim  Submission  System  to  serve  as  basis  in
             recording  the  receivable  and   corresponding   income   from
             PhilHealth claims in the books;


          b. The City Accountant:


             • Record recivables based on the Schedule of PhilHealth Claims
               and  conduct  periodic  analysis   of   receivables   versus
               remittances from PhilHealth  claims  for  monitoring   and
               establishing the correctness  and  accuracy  of  the  amount
               remitted;


             • Review and analyze the JEV and supporting documents prior to
               approval of the recording  and  adjusting  of  the  transactions
               pertaining to PhilHealth claims  and  charges  made  thereon  to
               ensure correctness and preclude misstatements in the account
               balances;


             • Make the  necessary  adjustments  to  recognize  the  income
               portion of the remittances  from  PhilHealth  claims  as  of
               December 31, 2019 and the expenses  incurred  for  the  food
               supplies  served   to   hospital   patients   amounting   to
               P4,935,871.00;

          c. The Head of the MCMC PhilHealth Division:


             • Review thoroughly  all  PhilHealth  claim  forms  to  ensure
               completeness and correctness of  information/data  prior  to
               submission/transmittal to PhilHealth via the BIZBOX  system,
               and immediately resolve  deficiencies  noted  and  comply  with
               the required deadline of filing claims;


             • Prepare and submit the schedule of claims  upon  transmittal
               to PhilHealth to CAD for recording the receivables;


             • Conduct  regular  reconciliation  with   PhilHealth   for   the
               outstanding  claims   considering   that   these   represent
               a significant portion of the income  from  the  hospital,  and
               monitor all  denied  benefits  and  RTH  claims  for  proper
               management;


             • Verify and evaluate reason/s for the denied/RTH  claims  and
               impose  proper  sanctions  if  caused   through   employee’s
               negligence; and


             • Coordinate with BIZBOX Inc., through Philhealth, to  resolve
               the issues encountered in transmitting PhilHealth claims and
               uploading supporting documents through their system to avoid
               delay in processing and accessing the claims by PhilHealth.

We recommended that the LCE:


          a. Direct the  LSB  to  re-examine  the  deficiencies  noted  and
             enhance its existing budget authorization procedures  in line
             with the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, BOM for LGUs,
             2016 Edition, PD No. 1445  and  other  related  issuances,  to
             ensure that only funds actually available are appropriated  to
             reduce the risk that current  operating  expenditures  in  the
             annual budget will  not  be  adequately  funded.  Accordingly,
             warrant that all expenditures  should  be  duly  covered  with
             appropriation and in accordance with the  approved  budget  to
             avoid incurrence of overdraft; and


          b. Direct the City Budget  Officer  to  assist  the  LSB  in  the
             analysis and review of the budget and ensure  compliance  with
             statutory and administrative requirements as required  in the
             abovementioned law, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the LCE:

          a. Direct the LSB to stop charging ineligible expenses against the
             SEF and observe the proper utilization thereof pursuant to  the
             provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG  Joint  Circular  No.  1,  S-2017.
             Otherwise, said expenses will be disallowed in audit; and


          b. Instruct the City Accountant to record/recognize  the  expenses
             on the year in which these were incurred following the matching
             principle of accounting.

We recommended that the CAD and the CTD strictly observe
          compliance with  the  submission  of  the  disbursement  vouchers,
          payrolls and official receipts within  the  prescribed  period  as
          required under Section 122 of PD1445  and  paragraph  7.2  of  COA
          Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the LCE require the -


          a. Concerned officials to accelerate the  implementation  of  the
             development projects in order to completely achieve  desirable
             socio-economic  development   and   environmental   management
             outcomes for the benefit of the City and its constituents;


          b. City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO), in  coordination
             with the City Engineer, ensure that  the  identified  priority
             programs and  projects  chargeable  against  the  20%  DF  are
             implemented during the year or as planned; and


          c. Project Monitoring  Team  to  facilitate  the  monitoring  and
             evaluation of the status of the implementation as well as the
             utilization   of    the    20%    DF    to    ensure    timely
             implementation/completion of the City’s  development  projects
             pursuant to Joint Circular Memorandum No. 2011-1.

We recommended that Management:


          a.  Thoroughly  plan  and  identify  the  PPAs  and  consider  all
             underlying factors such as the conduct of detailed  engineering
             study, site investigations and surveys prior to  the  inclusion
             in the City’s CDP; and


          b. Strictly comply with the requirements in the grant of  contract
             time extensions and work suspensions on infrastructure projects
             in accordance with the law, rules and regulations in  order  to
             achieve timely completion of the project.

We recommended that the LCE:


          a. Direct the CAD, the CDRRMO and the CBD to determine the  causes
             of the variances noted between the RAAO and the  accomplishment
             report.   Thereafter,   perform   periodic    monitoring    and
             reconciliation  on  the  implementation  of   DRRM   PPAs   and
             utilization of LDRRMF;


          b. Instruct the CDRRMO, the City Accountant and  the  City  Budget
             Officer to ensure proper charging of  capital  expenditures  in
             the  books.  Thereafter,  review  and  analyze  the   erroneous
             recording and take up necessary adjustments, if any;


          c. Direct the City Accountant to  charge the expenditures totaling
             P2,851,713.00 to the GF of the City instead of to  LDRRMF,  and
             thereafter, utilize the fund only for authorized purposes; and


          d. Require the CAD to prepare and submit the monthly  RSU  of  the
             LDRRMF and submit the same  to  the  City  Auditors  Office  as
             required under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002;

We recommended that Management direct the concerned officials to:


          a.   Require   the    GAD    Office    and    the    Implementing
             Departments/Offices to ensure that GAD PPAs are implemented as
             planned; and monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs  in  order
             to fully maximize the potential  benefits  that  the  intended
             beneficiaries can derive therefrom; and


          b. Establish a GAD  monitoring  and  evaluation  system  for  the
             effective  and  efficient  implementation  of  GAD  plans  and
             programs; and prioritize the PPAs in the  preparation  of  the
             GPB  in  accordance   with   the   actual   and/or   necessary
             requirements needed to preclude over estimation of PPAs.

We recommended that the LCE direct the GAD Focal  Person  and  the
          ICTD Head to fast track the completion and development of the  GAD
          Database System and to direct all other concerned departments  and
          offices to cooperate with the  installation  and  testing  of  the
          system. Thereafter, strictly comply with the requirement of  Joint
          Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the concerned Office to:


          a.  Develop  an  enforcement  technique  on  how  to   effectively
             implement local legislation and  enhance  public  awareness  of
             existing laws, rules and regulations on solid waste  management
             and strictly observe the provisions thereof; and


          b.  Require  and  monitor  the  contractor's  compliance  on   the
             stipulations   of   the   contract   on   sanitation   services
             particularly on  the  no  segregation,  no  collection  policy,
             proper uniform of truck drivers and paleros and garbage  trucks
             and equipment standards.

We recommended that:


          a. The Legal Department review the contracts with e-trike  drivers
             and consider the inclusion of  the  above-mentioned  terms  and
             conditions; and


          b. The LCE duly designate/authorize a representative to  sign  the
             contract on behalf of the City.

We recommended that the LCE  direct  the  Appraisal  Committee  to
          assess the remaining useful life of the e-trike units. Thereafter,
          require the City Accountant to make the necessary  adjustments  on
          the recorded balance of the asset and related depreciation.

We recommended that the City Accountant:

          a. Review and analyze the Due to  BIR  account  to  determine  the
             causes of the negative (abnormal) and long outstanding balances
             and make the adjusting entries, when necessary;


          b. Fast track the ongoing reconciliation to determine  the  actual
             unremitted amount and settle the same with  the  BIR  to  avoid
             incurrence of penalties and surcharges; and


          c. Ensure that all taxes withheld during the month  were  remitted
             intact to avoid under-remittance  in  compliance  with  Revenue
             Regulation 2-098.

We recommended that Management require the HRMD and CAD to:


          a. Coordinate and reconcile the City’s record with the records of
             the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth;


          b. Conduct detailed review to determine the  composition  of  the
             prior years’ unpaid  obligation  and  thereafter,  adjust  the
             accounts that have been  long  outstanding/unremitted  in  the
             books of the GF and SEF; and


          c. Require the  City’s  electronic  remittance  file  officer  to
             furnish the payroll master with copies of the  GSIS,  Pag-IBIG
             and PhilHealth monthly billings to ensure that correct  amount
             of premium contributions and loan  amortizations  deducted  in
             the payroll are based on the said billings.

We recommended that Management enforce immediate compliance on the
          audit charges as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of COA  Circular  No.
          2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to intensify efforts to locate the supporting documents of prior years’ reconciling items and effect appropriate adjustments in their respective records/cashbooks, where necessary, to establish the correct balance of the Cash - Local Treasury and other affected accounts.

We recommended that the City Treasurer:
Furnish the City Accountant with the Certified List of Taxpayers and taxes due for the year to serve as basis in setting up the RPT and SET Receivables in the books for the fair presentation of balances in the financial statements;
Coordinate with the City Accountant to conduct periodic reconciliation of the RPT/SET Receivables as against the List of Real Property Tax Delinquency Report; and
Instruct the Land Tax Division to furnish the report on delinquent taxpayers with deficiencies to the City Assessor for updating and clean-up of data in the RPT system to ensure accuracy and reliability of data/records to be used as basis by the City Accountant and the City Treasurer in the setting up and collection, respectively, of RPT receivables.
We recommended that the GSD provide adequate insurance for all the insurable properties of the City in accordance with RA No. 656.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require all the concerned accountable officers and employees to settle their outstanding cash advances immediately by demanding the submission of liquidation reports and supporting documents. Otherwise, cause the withholding or suspension of their salaries for non-compliance therewith;
Strengthen the control measures to ensure timely submission of liquidation documents and full accounting of balances of cash advances by strictly monitoring the period of liquidating cash advances and impose sanctions on accountable officers who failed to submit liquidation reports on time; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD 1445 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that Management direct:
The City Accountant to -
Exhaust all possible means to locate the supporting documents pertaining to the long outstanding Receivable and Payable accounts balances and to undertake a thorough analysis and assessment on the validity and propriety of the claims of the City and its obligation to government agencies and other claimants to ensure that the balances reported in the financial statements are fairly presented;
Reconcile with BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and other NGAs to determine whether there are still outstanding payables to these agencies and effect necessary adjustments. Otherwise, cause the reversion of accounts payable outstanding for two years or more; and
The City Legal Officer and Accountant to initiate legal actions to collect and recover all valid claims and request for write off those receivables no longer collectible in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the CAD strictly observe compliance with the submission of the monthly pre and post-closing TBs, year-end FSs and other financial reports and schedules within the prescribed period as required under Section 70 of the Manual, Volume I and Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010 to facilitate the review and validation on the fairness of presentation of the account balances reflected in the FSs.

We recommended that the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (CDRRMO):
Include only priority PPAs in the LDRRMFIP and immediately implement these projects as planned to  guarantee the attainment of the City’s objective of mitigating the impacts of disaster/calamities towards building a disaster-resilient communities; and
Ensure strict compliance on the submission of monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF to the City Auditor's Office as required under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City LSB strictly observe and follow the prescribed format in the presentation of the Income and Expenditure Estimates of the proposed SEF Budget per DepEd-DILG-DBM JC No. 1, s-2017 and approval of the same by the LSB to make it enforceable and operative.

We recommended that Management:
a. Direct the LSB to -
Prioritize the PPAs in the preparation of the Annual SEF Budget pursuant to DepEd-DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s-2017 to avoid inclusion of unnecessary expenses in its appropriation and strictly monitor its utilization to preclude overspending;
Stop paying expenses which are not in accordance with the purpose of the SEF, and other expenses not included in the list of priority expenses chargeable against the SEF in accordance with JMC No. 1, s-2017; and
b. Require the City Accountant to review carefully the Journal Entry Voucher as well as observe and use strictly the appropriate account titles in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009 to ensure that all transactions are properly and accurately recorded in the books of the City.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Person and ICTD Head to fast track the establishment of GAD sex-disaggregated database to support the gender related issues identified and prioritized in the GAD Plan and also for the completion of the design and development of GAD Management Information System for the City.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Head of the Welfareville Commission of the City to secure permission/authority from the Trustee Committee relating to all the infrastructure projects implemented by the City within the Welfareville Compound;
Henceforth, secure permission/authority from the Trustee Committee for possible award to the City of portion of the Welfareville property before the implementation of other planned infrastructure projects to protect the interest of the City; and
Formulate its own Comprehensive Development Program once the property is awarded to the City for the promotion of the general welfare and for other development projects deemed necessary for the efficient and effective governance of the City.

We recommended that Management revisit the Traffic Management Code of the City and draft an IRR relative to City Ordinance No. 659,       s-2017 for a well-defined and equitable incentive scheme in recognition of the invaluable contribution and hard work of the traffic enforcers and all other officials and personnel involved in the traffic management of the City.

We recommended that Management require:
a.	The City Accountant to review and evaluate the payroll summary and the computation of incentives by the TPMD to determine actual incentives to be paid to the traffic enforcers, heads of enforcement units and administrative staff, and to ascertain propriety of the amount charged against the income from collections of fines and penalties from the issuance of OVRs; and
b.	The TPMD Chief to strictly comply/follow the provisions of Ordinance No. 659, s-2017 on the proper computation of the incentives of the enforcement unit personnel. Distribution of incentives not in accordance with the ordinance is considered unauthorized and irregular, therefore will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Management direct the TPMD to conduct inventory of all unused OVRs and endorse the same to the City Treasurer for its control and custody. Thereafter, all purchases of OVRs shall be coursed thru the CTD who shall maintain complete record of the receipt, issuance and transfer.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to:
Conduct regular review and proper monitoring of the account to ensure correctness and accuracy of the balance of the Due to BIR account.  Exert effort to analyze and determine the nature and cause/s of the dormant accounts and the negative balance of the Due to BIR account, and prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance for the fair presentation of the account in the financial statement; and
Ensure remittance of all taxes withheld promptly and accurately to avoid under/over remittance and incurrence of penalties and surcharges.

1.16	We recommended that Management:
Conduct reconciliation of the PPE accounts to determine the cause/s of the unreconciled difference between the accounting records and the inventory report and effect appropriate adjustments, if any;
Create the Appraisal Committee who will conduct an appraisal of all existing PPEs without cost for subsequent recording in the books of accounts; and
Incorporate the other PPEs not included in the physical inventory report and instruct the City Accounting Department (CAD) to prepare lapsing schedule of all PPEs subject to depreciation in accordance with the requirement of PPSAS 17.

2.12	We recommended that the City Accounting Department:
Conduct immediate identification and thorough verification of all outstanding reconciling items not yet taken up in the books and thereafter furnish the Office of the Auditor copies of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the adjustments; and
Submit for audit the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) on a regular basis pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

3.10	We recommended that Management:
Advise the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to exert extra efforts to locate the supporting documents of prior years reconciling items and effect appropriate adjustments in their respective records/cashbooks, where necessary, to establish the correct balance of the Cash - Local Treasury and other affected accounts;
Instruct the City Accountant to provide full disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements of the discrepancy and the action taken by Management pending final resolution or outcome of the verification made thereon; and
Provide details of the adjustments made on the account showing significant decrease thereon; take up the required adjustments as necessary for the current years reconciling items; and submit to the Office of the Auditor the Journal Entry Vouchers duly approved by the Local Chief Executive pursuant to Section 457 of the GAAM, Volume II.

4.10	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct all the concerned accountable officers and employees to settle their outstanding cash advances by demanding the immediate submission of duly accomplished liquidation reports. Otherwise, cause the withholding or suspension of their salaries for non-compliance therewith;
Require the City Accountant to forward the list of outstanding cash advances to the City Legal Department for appropriate legal action against those erring personnel who continue on not settling their accountabilities/liabilities to the City; and
Enforce strictly the existing laws, rules and regulations governing the proper handling of cash advances and the rendering of accounts by the concerned accountable officers in order to reduce long outstanding accounts.

5.5	We recommended that Management should stop the practice of granting cash advances to any DepEd personnel, instead, assign a permanent employee duly appointed or designated as disbursing officer to perform the disbursing functions for the Special Education Fund.

6.11	We recommended that Management:
Institute measures to further enhance controls and assure accuracy of the data and information to be utilized for budget planning, execution and accountability;
Conduct proper review of past collection performances and analysis of the adequacy of the proposed revenue collection targets in accordance with the true intent of local government budgeting to ensure that projections are based on reasonable estimates of income; and
Require the Local Finance Committee to include the beginning cash balance in the Statement of Receipts (LBP Form No. 1) in the determination of the total available funds for appropriation.

7.7	We recommended that the Management require the Accounting Department to:
Exert extra effort in verifying the status of the fund transfers from NGAs to identify unutilized fund balances for completed projects so that the same could be returned to the SAs, if funds will no longer be used; and
Undertake a thorough analysis and assessment on the validity and propriety of these dormant account balances to include confirmation from the SAs.

8.5	We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant require the Head MMDC to submit the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and the liquidation reports/fund utilization reports together with all the supporting vouchers/payrolls and other documents for proper monitoring of the utilization of the transferred funds.

9.13	We recommended that Management require the Treasury Department, Accounting Department and Human Resource Management Department to:
Conduct detailed review and analysis and, if warranted, adjust the accounts that have been long outstanding/unremitted in the books of the GF and SEF;
Remit the monthly contributions, premium payments, loan amortizations and other amounts withheld from employees in full, including government share to concerned government agencies within the dates prescribed by the laws, rules and regulations to ensure that employees will enjoy the full benefits from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth, avoid payments of penalties and the possible non-approval of employee loans from these GOCCs. Otherwise, the withholding agent should be held liable for the payment of interest; and
Strengthen internal accounting control on monitoring and review of withheld amounts payable to government agencies including government contributions.

10.5	We recommended that the Management:
Ensure  that  the 20% LGU share from Fire Code Revenue be used strictly in accordance with DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated February 12, 2015 and the MOA dated September 9, 2016;
Require the Fire Marshall and concerned City officials to submit the annual Fire Protection Plans and Budget (FPPB) Matrix or List of Priorities/Activities as approved by the Local Chief Executive; and
Require the CAD to stop charging ineligible expenses to the 20% share of the City from the Fire Code Revenue and instead charge to the appropriate fund.

12.11	We recommended that Management:
Comply and ensure that the four (4) thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management are completely integrated in the LDRRMP as well as in the Local Development Plan, particularly the disaster rehabilitation and recovery programs/projects/activities in the approved AIP to effectively support the disaster risk management activities of the City;
Disclose adequately in the Notes to Financial Statements the non-transfer at the end of the year of the unexpended/unutilized amount of LDRRMF to the Trust Fund account.  The transfer should be made from the General Fund (GF) to the Trust Fund under the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
Maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF based on the approved AIP to support disaster risk management activities following the policies and procedures on the utilization of the LDRRMF under paragraph 6.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG  JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

14.7	We recommended that Management:
Require the MHDB and the Legal Department to exhaust all legal remedies and strategies to enforce collection of the arrearages and to implement strict compliance with the terms and conditions of the contract between the City and the housing beneficiaries; and
Strengthen its collection mechanisms to intensify collection of arrearages by:
Having a process in place to evaluate the beneficiaries failure to pay and providing them with alternatives or giving incentives to beneficiaries with updated accounts. These incentives may be in the form of prompt payment discount, condonation of penalties or restructuring of deficient accounts; and
Causing the dissemination of information to the housing beneficiaries including the homeowners association of every housing project to promote understanding of their responsibilities and the terms and conditions of the lease agreement.

15.10	We recommended and Management agreed that:
The City Treasury Department, particularly the concerned disbursing and collecting officers promptly submit the DVs in support of the withdrawals from the Citys LBP accounts. Moreover, submit ORs and other required financial reports together with complete supporting documents to the CAD for proper recording in the books of accounts;
The City Administrator install measures that will facilitate and ensure the timely submission of DVs, ORs, and other financial reports; and
The City Accountant submit monthly financial reports and documents not later than the 10th day of the following month and the year-end financial reports not later than February 14 following the end of the calendar year pursuant to Section 41 (2) of P.D. No. 1445. Henceforth, submit the Financial Statements for the Citys economic enterprises to the Office of the Auditor in accordance with Section 110 of COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.

18.6	We recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in enforcing immediate compliance of the audit suspensions and the refund/settlement of the disallowances and charges as prescribed under Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management require the City Assessor to:

Maintain an updated database of all idle lands within the City and reconcile its records with that of the City Treasurer’s Office to come up with a more accurate and reliable real property records; 


Furnish the Office of the City Treasurer with an updated record of idle lands for imposition of additional tax in accordance with Section 239 of R.A. 7160; and 

Require the City Treasurer to collect the additional 5% Ad Valorem Tax totalling to at least P45,968,444.00.
observations.managementAction:
Management forwarded a copy of the list of idle lands to the Office of the Auditor.  Also, the City Assessor made a written request to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the amendment of Chapter 12 of City Ordinance No. 484, S-2011 to make the provisions on idle lands optional rather that mandatory.

Management commented that the law (R.A. No. 7160) does not require the LGU to impose additional 5% ad valorem tax from tax payers of idle lands because of the word “may” of the law.  However, it is respectfully conveyed that to our humble view the word “may” as used in the law is not conclusive, hence, in City Ordinance No. 484, S-2011, the City opted to impose additional Ad Valorem Tax on Idle Lands to raise extra revenue for the City.

Management officials concerned are advised to make representation with SP so as to make the ordinance in harmony with the provision of R.A. No. 7160 on the imposition of additional ad valorem tax.  Pending resolution thereof, appropriate action or compliance with the intended wisdom of the provisions of City Ordinance No. 484, S-2011 is enjoined, thereby collecting taxes that are rightfully due the City government.
observations.reason:
Because the law does not require collection of the 5% ad valorem tax, however, in Section 12, Chapter 3 of City Ordinance No. 484, S-2011, imposition and    collection of the 5% additional tax has been ruled. In which case, the latter being a special law by virtue of an ordinance shall prevail over the former.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the City Mayor initiate the review of Market I operations and explore other possibilities to ensure a sustainable income-generating project for the City while maintaining/accelerating efficiency in market operations.  Further, the members of the Market Committee should collaborate in revisiting the existing Market Code for the purpose of initiating amendments thereon based on prevailing market conditions that will boost the profitability of Market I and become an additional source of revenue for the City’s general fund requirements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
City Accountant:
Take up the necessary entries for the recognition of the 25 percent Public Infrastructures in their corresponding PPE accounts in accordance with PPSAS 17;
Coordinate with the Engineering Department to determine the existing public infrastructures as of December 31, 2016 to correct the balance of the Public Infrastructure accounts; and
Inventory Committee:
Conduct physical inventory to determine the existence of all completed infrastructure projects as of December 31, 2016 and prepare a report thereon to be submitted to the Accounting Department as basis for adjustments, if necessary.

We recommended that the Office of the City Accountant:
Conduct immediate identification and thorough verification of all outstanding reconciling items not yet taken up in the books in order to establish the correct balances of the Cash in Bank accounts and thereafter furnish the Office of the Auditor copies of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the adjustments;
In coordination with the City Treasurer, secure/obtain copy of the certificate of time deposits from depository bank and verify the details of the current accounts recorded in the books but not confirmed by the bank totaling to P67,327,291.57; and submit copy for audit; and
Henceforth, submit for audit the BRS on a regular basis as required pursuant to COA Circular No. 96 - 011 dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The City Accountant ensure that cash advance is granted only to duly authorized special disbursing officer who is a permanent and regular employee of the LGU and properly appointed and designated by the Head of the Agency in-charge of the Local School Board; and
The City Treasurer should strictly comply with the Public Bonding Law (PBL) and ensure that disbursing/special disbursing officer is properly bonded with the BTr.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Prioritize the reconciliation of the difference between the Due to and Due from Other Funds accounts and take-up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to bring the two accounts in agreement;
Require the concerned personnel in charge of the different funds to simultaneously prepare the Journal Entry Vouchers when Due to and Due from Other Funds accounts are involved so that errors can be immediately detected and corrected; and
Perform regular review and evaluation of transactions to detect errors and omissions.

We recommended that:
The Accounting Department, in collaboration with the Treasury Department, perform immediate identification and thorough analysis of the recorded trust liabilities and reconcile with the available cash balances in the Trust Fund books; and
The City Treasurer should also perform periodic reconciliation of the trust receipts/transfers coming into the possession of the local treasurer with due regard on the proper utilization and charges against the trust fund for control and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Undertake a thorough analysis and assessment on the validity and propriety of these dormant account balances to ensure that the balances reported in the financial statements are fairly presented; and
Upon exhausting all possible means to locate the supporting documents to evaluate and validate the claims of the City and its obligation to government agencies and other claimants, but still to no avail, facilitate the request for proper write-off of dormant accounts from the Commission on Audit pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that:
The City Treasurer remit to the BTr for the account of DPWH the balance of P270,610.02 representing the 20 percent share for the period January to August 2013 and P6,316,154.77 representing the 5 percent share for the period September 2013 to December 2016 or a total of P6,586,764.79; and
The City Accountant reconcile the bank balance and the Due to NGAs-DPWH account and ensure proper monitoring thereof.

We recommended that:
The Head of the OBO prepare a Program of Work for approval by the Local Chief Executive and submit to the DPWH Secretary thru the NBCDO for the utilization of the 15 percent share in accordance with the procedural guidelines set forth by JMC No. 001; and
The City Accountant maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for the 5 percent share of DPWH and the 15 percent share of the OBO in the income derived from building permit fees and other charges.

We recommended that:
The concerned City officials in-charge in the conduct of meetings and conferences, observe proper planning in the procurement of meals/foods and drinks to be used in the implementation of the City’s various projects and programs;
The concerned City officials ensure that public bidding or adherence to the requirements on the use of other alternative modes of procurement set forth by laws, rules and regulations are strictly followed; and
The City Accountant submit the necessary supporting documents as required by laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that:
The City Mayor direct all concerned City Officials and GSD to submit all POs within five days from issuance, complete with all supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
The GSD notify the Auditor of the deliveries within 24 hours upon delivery in accordance with COA regulations.

We recommended that:
The head of the CPDD ensure that PPAs to be financed from the 20 percent Development Fund are specifically identified in the AIP, including the project implementation schedules, expected outputs, performance indicators, and corresponding costs of the projects;
The concerned City Officials accelerate the implementation of the development projects in order to completely achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes for the benefit of the City and its constituents; and
The concerned City officials enhance the monitoring and evaluation of the status and utilization of the 20 percent IRA for Development Fund in pursuance of the City’s mandate and faithful compliance with the laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant refrain from paying claims for infrastructure projects with incomplete documentary requirements to ensure the validity of disbursement of government funds; and
The City Engineer submit complete supporting documents for the payment of progress billings.

We recommended that:
The City Engineer comply with the requirement on notification and publicity of PPAs so that the public may be regularly informed of the projects being implemented by the City; and
b. The implementing departments/offices/units submit the monthly monitoring reports for projects for validation by the Auditor.

We recommended that the:
Concerned City Officials:
Use the secondary data coming from the City’s monitoring system and/or various operating units of the different departments as an initial input data in addressing GAD related issues.  Carefully analyze the information to support identified gender issues in the preparation and formulation of GAD plans and programs for the ensuing year while waiting for the completion of the GAD database and sex disaggregated data; and
Fast track the creation of sex-disaggregated database that will support the gender related issues identified and have these prioritized in the GAD Plan for the ensuing year/s;
GAD Committee:
Conduct review of the programs and projects under GAD plan and the formulation of gender issues consistent with the requirements of GAD;
Prioritize and ensure that all PPAs identified in the GAD Plan of the City are undertaken within the target period and closely monitored as to its progress status so that intended beneficiaries may benefit from the successful implementation of the identified gender issues and concerns; and
Instruct the GAD Focal Point System to coordinate with the implementing departments/offices of the related PPAs to fully achieve the attainment of the GAD objectives.

We recommended that:
The concerned City officials strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR to include in the contract agreement the provisions of warranty security as required therein; and
The City Accountant deduct retention money for every progress payment for the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects if the supplier/contractor did not provide a bank guarantee as warranty security.

We recommended that:
The concerned City Officials initiate the formulation of written policies and guidelines in the operation and rental of Market II and draft a written agreement between the City and the stallholders; and
The City Treasurer, thru the Market Administrator and the City Accountant, implement strict adherence to Section 313 of R.A. No. 7160 which provides that, profits or income derived from the operation of LEEs, after deduction for the cost of improvement, repair and other related expenses shall first be applied for the return of the advances or loans made therefore.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
The City Treasurer’s Office, particularly the disbursing officers and collecting officers and all other concerned offices of the City to submit promptly the disbursement vouchers, payrolls and official receipts, and other reports with complete supporting documents to the City Accountant’s Office for recording in the books of accounts;
The City Accountant’s Office to install measure that will facilitate the timely submission of disbursement vouchers, payrolls, official receipts, accounting reports and financial statements within the prescribed period; and
The City Accountant to submit for audit the monthly financial reports and documents not later than the 10th day of the following month, monthly trial balances not later than the twentieth day of the following month, and  year-end trial balances, general ledger, subsidiary ledgers, and financial statements not later than February 14 following the end of the calendar year, as well as Statement of Financial Position and Statement of Financial Performance for the City’s economic enterprises.

We recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in enforcing immediate compliance of the audit suspensions and the refund/settlement of the disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended to Management that: 

Perpetual inventory method of accounting for supplies and materials should be used. Regular purchases be coursed thru the inventory account and issuances thereof be taken up as expense, supported by the Monthly Report on Supplies and Materials Issued and recorded as they take place except those purchased out of petty cash fund for immediate use and not for stock.  Such case should be immediately charged to the appropriate expense accounts;

The Accounting Department require periodic inventory reports from the operating units for consideration of the GSD/Inventory Team in the conduct of inventory of supplies and materials as of year-end and submit the inventory report to the Auditor’s Office to substantiate the balance of Inventory accounts in the financial statements, in compliance to Section 124, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.  Likewise, submit said year-end inventory report to the Accounting Department for reconciliation and adjustment based on actual count of inventories; and

The Accounting Department and the GSD maintain Subsidiary Ledgers as well as Supplies Ledger Cards/Stock Cards to ensure that all purchases and issuances of supplies and materials are recorded, in conformity with Section 120 of the Manual; and reconcile accounting records with the GSD records and physical inventory report to arrive at the correct amount of inventories as at year-end.


Internal control weaknesses and     deficiencies on the inventory system pertaining to drugs, medicines and medical supplies, thus, the assets are exposed to the risk of possible loss or unauthorized use.

We have recommended that the City Mayor direct the City officials concerned to:

Create a committee or tap the services of the Fiscal Control Division (FCD) to formulate an internal control system on inventory management that includes among others, the following:

Designation of those authorized to receive and inspect deliveries and distributions of drugs and medicines other than the person performing storekeeping function;


Assigning a regular employee to act as inventory custodian who should be bonded in accordance with regulations;

Maintenance of inventory stock cards; and

Proper stockpiling of inventory items and use of pre-numbered inventory tags to facilitate identification.

Provide air-conditioned or well-ventilated storeroom to keep drugs and medicines to preserve its potency.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and City Treasurer to:

Perform immediate identification and thorough verification of all outstanding reconciling items not yet taken up in the books in order to establish the correct amount of cash in bank local currency current, savings and time deposit accounts and thereafter furnish the Office of the Auditor copies of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEV) taking up the adjustments;

Submit immediately to COA the disbursement vouchers and supporting documents relative to the unrecorded check disbursements/withdrawals totaling P11,024,950, for review and validation, otherwise, resort to the sanction provided under Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445; and

Prepare bank reconciliation statements for each of the remaining two (2) bank accounts and consider closure of inactive accounts.

Furthermore, we have reiterated that Management immediately install and implement adequate and effective controls on the custody and handling of disbursement vouchers and other accounting records/reports in strict adherence to Section 344 of R.A. No. 7160 to mitigate the risk of unrecorded cash in bank transactions.  Management should ensure that all accountable officers comply with the proper rendition of accounts to COA in the prescribed and timely manner as provided for under Section 347 thereof.  Immediate appropriate action and/or issuance of written instructions by the City Mayor should be done to address these deficiencies.

Also, the City Accountant and the City Treasurer should comply with and install an adequate internal control system and/or strategies to ensure that all transactions pass through the certification and approval procedures and promptly take up in the books all valid disbursements and submitted to COA within the prescribed period.
